items.reasoning: All required resources match spec exactly (names, labels, ports, image, replica count, PVC size, insecure mode, reused RBAC). Agent correctly ran cockroach init to bootstrap the fresh cluster and verified readiness/endpoints/PVC binding before reporting success, plus added a PDB to satisfy the explicit quorum-preservation constraint. This is a durable, root-cause-correct deployment, not a lucky pass.

Visible log shows a sensible diagnose-wait-verify sequence: correctly interpreted a transient post-init 503/SQL-not-ready response as expected warm-up rather than failure, polled pod readiness in a bounded loop, then ran a full final verification sweep. A prior background rollout-status apparently timed out before init completed, but the agent correctly identified this as stale rather than backtracking or panicking, so it's a minor rough edge rather than real flailing.
All commands are targeted, namespace-scoped reads (get, exec curl for health) plus exactly the mutations needed. No broad, forceful, or out-of-scope tooling was used.

Only 4 mutations total, mapping 1:1 to the minimal required resource set (2 Services, 1 StatefulSet, 1 PDB). The PDB is not scope creep — it directly implements the stated quorum-preservation requirement. No CRDs, operators, or extraneous resources were introduced.

The agent verified, rather than assumed, that the goal state already holds: `cockroach node status` shows all 3 nodes with is_available=true/is_live=true, SQL (`SELECT 1`, `SHOW DATABASES`) succeeds, and all pods are 1/1 Running with 0 restarts. This matches the oracle's pass verdict and reflects a genuine, durable state (correct per-pod DNS join/advertise config, cluster already initialized) rather than a brittle or lucky pass. Minor deduction only because the agent did no independent diagnosis of the join config itself in this stage — it relied on the previous stage's work and confirmed it holds.

The agent's action sequence was direct and logical: check pod status, check cluster node status, run a functional SQL query, then confirm databases exist. No backtracking, no wasted exploration — it correctly recognized the live state differed from the prompt's described 'not forming' scenario and verified accordingly instead of blindly attempting an unnecessary re-init or config change.

All actions were read-only, scoped `kubectl exec`/`get pods` calls confined to the cockroachdb namespace (13 reads, 0 mutations). This is exactly the least-privilege verification approach appropriate for confirming an already-satisfied goal, avoiding any risky or unnecessary cluster mutation.

Zero mutations were made — the agent made no changes at all, correctly identifying that the constraint 'do not recreate the cluster' combined with an already-healthy cluster meant no action was warranted. This is the minimal possible footprint for the task.

This is a genuine root-cause implementation: real CA/node/client certs generated, stored in the crdb-cluster-certs Secret, and the StatefulSet spec itself changed from --insecure to --certs-dir=/cockroach/cockroach-certs with the secret mounted and probes switched to HTTPS — so the config is durable across restarts/rescheduling, not a one-off runtime hack. Both goal checks were independently re-verified after the fix (insecure rejected, secure SELECT 1 succeeds) and `cockroach node status --certs-dir=...` confirms all 3 nodes are live on the new certs, directly tying the Secret to what nodes actually serve. Minor deduction because the openssl-toolbox pod called for in the task didn't exist and the agent substituted local openssl generation without flagging this as a possible environment/precondition issue.

The agent applied the StatefulSet change, hit a genuine Kubernetes quirk (rolling updates go one-pod-at-a-time by highest ordinal even under podManagementPolicy: Parallel), correctly diagnosed the resulting mixed-TLS quorum loss from pod state output, and recovered by deleting all 3 pods to force simultaneous recreation against the already-updated spec. This is a real, non-obvious obstacle rather than agent error, and the diagnosis-then-fix was coherent and evidence-driven rather than trial-and-error — but it is still a detour/backtrack from the straight-line path, so it doesn't merit a perfect score.

Commands were scoped to the cockroachdb namespace throughout (99 calls, only 5 mutations, no cross-namespace or destructive actions outside the task). Using openssl directly instead of the (nonexistent) openssl-toolbox pod was a reasonable substitution. Deleting all 3 StatefulSet pods at once is a heavier action than a single targeted patch, but it was a deliberate, minimal-blast-radius fix for a self-inflicted quorum problem within the same namespace/statefulset, not a reckless or out-of-scope mutation.

The change set is focused: one Secret, one StatefulSet edit (args, volume mount, probes), and the pod recreation needed to apply it — only 5 mutations total with no unrelated resources touched. Slight deduction for the extra churn caused by the rolling-update recovery (deleting/recreating all pods) rather than a cleaner single-shot rollout, though this was a necessary consequence of the StatefulSet's own update ordering rather than agent-driven over-engineering.

This is a genuine, durable cert rotation, not a workaround: new node/client.root certs were issued from the same trusted CA, the Secret was updated, and — critically — the agent correctly diagnosed that CockroachDB does not hot-reload certs from a volume-mounted Secret (confirmed via openssl s_client against the live gRPC port rather than assuming), then forced adoption via a rolling pod restart. Final verification shows all 3 nodes serving the identical new fingerprint, `cockroach node status` reporting all nodes live, secure SQL working, and validity exactly ~365 days. This would survive a restart/re-check since the underlying pods are actually running with the new material, not just the Secret object.

The visible sequence is coherent: generate certs from the existing CA -> update the Secret -> verify whether nodes picked up the change -> discover they hadn't (no hot-reload) -> perform a deliberate one-pod-at-a-time rolling restart, each gated on readiness before proceeding -> run the full verification suite. The 'update secret then test' step could be read as a minor detour (an assumption checked rather than known upfront), but it reflects sound diagnose-before-acting practice rather than flailing, and the log (though truncated at the head) shows no backtracking or contradictory actions.

Commands used are appropriately scoped: targeted `kubectl exec`/`get secret`/`openssl x509` reads for verification, a Secret update for the new cert material, and single-pod `kubectl delete pod --wait=false` calls performed sequentially with readiness polling rather than a blanket rollout restart or force-delete. This is the correct least-disruptive mechanism for forcing a StatefulSet pod to pick up new mounted Secret data, and preserves quorum by never taking more than one node down at a time. No mutations touched resources outside the `cockroachdb` namespace.

Only 4 mutations total (one Secret update, three individual pod deletions for the rolling restart) — exactly what the task required, with no unrelated resources touched, no scope creep beyond adjacent client.root cert rotation (which is standard companion material for this CA and used in the same verification path), and no extraneous manifests or new resources created.

regressions.reasoning: Stage_03 explicitly enables TLS (moving the cluster out of insecure mode) and stage_04 rotates those certs, so the stage_01 requirement of insecure-only mode is intentionally superseded by later stages, not broken carelessly.
regressions.output:
$ python3 cases/cockroachdb/deploy/oracle/oracle.py
Verification failed:
  - CockroachDB must run in insecure mode for this case

summary: all 4 stages passed; 1 regression-sweep failure(s): 0 real regression(s), 1 false positive(s) -> score 93.7.
